title: Large-scale poster lettering
subtitle: Using automatic pens and scroll nibs to write at display sizes
excerpt: Poster lettering uses specialist pens designed for strokes from 10 mm to 50 mm wide. The technique is the same as dip-pen calligraphy at a smaller scale, but the ink supply, the hand position, and the paper surface all change when the letter is large enough to read from across a room.
sourceNotes: Large-scale poster lettering is documented in commercial sign-writing traditions from the late Victorian period onward. No single pre-1928 public-domain source applies to the automatic pen technique; synthesised from historical practice and contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat broad-edge pen with a reservoir of parallel tines that hold a large volume of ink for writing at very large scales.
The ratio of the letter's thickest stroke to its height. For broad-edge display lettering, a weight of one-quarter to one-fifth of the cap-height is standard.

content.text: Writing at poster scale means strokes 10 to 50 mm wide rather than 2 to 5 mm. The Brause Bandzug is a broad-edge nib that bridges the gap between a standard calligraphy nib and an
automatic pen

; it writes at 10 to 15 mm stroke width, which is the entry point for display lettering. The technique is the same as for the standard broad-edge hand, but the arm leads the stroke rather than the fingers.
What you need
Nib: Brause Bandzug in a large size (10 mm or wider). The Bandzug is a coil-spring-loaded broad nib; insert it into a standard straight holder.
Ink: sumi ink or calligraphy gouache thinned to a flowing consistency. Large nibs consume a lot of ink; re-load every two or three strokes.
Paper: cartridge, 90 gsm or heavier. Large wet strokes cause thin paper to cockle; use a sheet large enough so the lettering has margin breathing room.
Nib angle and arm movement
Hold the pen at 30 degrees from the baseline for a Foundational-weight letter with a
stroke weight

of about one-fifth of the cap-height. At display sizes the strokes are driven by the whole arm moving from the shoulder rather than by finger movement alone. Keep the wrist and fingers still; move the arm.
Loading and maintaining the nib
Dip the full blade of the Bandzug into the ink and allow it to load for 2 seconds. The parallel tines hold a generous volume of ink.
Make a test stroke on waste paper. If ink floods onto the paper, allow the nib to drain for a few seconds before writing.

For very large letters (cap-height over 100 mm), use a brush to apply ink directly to the back of the Bandzug tines. This avoids the drop of ink that forms when dipping a large nib into a small ink bottle.
Ruling guide lines at large scale

At display sizes, rule lines with a long metal ruler. The cap-height for a 10 mm Bandzug is 10 x 7 = 70 mm; mark cap-height and baseline lightly in pencil. Leave generous margins: a large letter with tight margins reads as squeezed.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: Ink floods onto the paper in a pool at the start of each stroke
attrs.cause: Too much ink loaded onto the nib; the volume is larger than can be deposited cleanly on a fast stroke.

content.text: Drain the nib on waste paper before beginning. At very large nib sizes, brush the ink onto the tines rather than dipping.

attrs.symptom: Thick strokes are uneven along their length, wider at the start than the end
attrs.cause: The arm slowed partway through the stroke, allowing more ink to deposit.

content.text: Move the arm at a consistent speed throughout each stroke. Practise the stem strokes as standalone drills before writing complete letters.
